title: Unit 3 Checkpoint Quiz — Ser vs. Estar

questions.prompt: Which verb do you use to talk about someone's profession?
questions.explanation: Professions are permanent defining characteristics, so they use 'ser'. Example: Soy maestro.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct verb: El banco ___ cerrado los domingos.
questions.explanation: A current condition or state uses 'estar'. Being closed is a changeable condition, not a defining characteristic.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'These tacos are delicious!'?
options: Both A and C are correct.
questions.explanation: 'Están ricos' and 'están riquísimos' are both correct — estar is used for food taste. 'Son ricos' would imply the tacos are wealthy!

options: Soy de Oaxaca y estoy cansado.
Estoy de Oaxaca y soy cansado.
Estoy de Oaxaca y estoy cansado.
Soy de Oaxaca y soy cansado.
questions.explanation: Origin uses ser (soy de Oaxaca). Tiredness is a temporary state, so it uses estar (estoy cansado).

questions.prompt: 'Es aburrido' vs. 'Está aburrido' — what's the difference?
options: Es aburrido = He is a boring person; Está aburrido = He is bored right now
Es aburrido = He is bored right now; Está aburrido = He is a boring person
Es aburrido = He is boring (thing); Está aburrido = He is boring (place)
questions.explanation: With ser, adjectives describe permanent traits. With estar, they describe temporary states. 'Es aburrido' = boring personality; 'Está aburrido' = feeling bored right now.

options: Son las tres de la tarde.
Están las tres de la tarde.
questions.explanation: Time always uses 'ser'. Son las tres = It is three o'clock. (Plural because 'las horas' is implied.)
'Chido/chida' is very common Mexican slang meaning cool or awesome. It uses 'estar' because it describes a current impression.

options: La mesa es en el comedor.
questions.explanation: Material (what something is made of) uses ser. 'La mesa es de madera' = The table is made of wood. Location would use estar: La mesa está en el comedor.
